## Real Material - Architect Learning Build (Data Engineering: Lakehouse ETL Governance)

This section is the study-first architecture guide for databricks, delta lake, unity catalog, glue, and etl governance patterns.

### Phase 1 - Foundations

#### Module: Lakehouse ETL Governance Mental Model
- Use lakehouse etl governance decisions to shape blast radius, access paths, and operational ownership.
- Study service behavior first, then map it into production architecture and exam-ready design language.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- Even though hive Metastore was widely adopted and had been used by customers for over seven years, it has some limitations such as lack of fine grained access control like row and column level access, lack of audit logging, lineage support for multi-cloud environments, etc. so Databricks introduced Unity catalog around late 2022 to address some of the limitations of this solution.
- So I've already got the DDL for creating a Delta table, depending on which version of Databricks you're using, Delta is now default, so if you're using Databricks version 8 onwards, you don't have to specify Delta because it is the default, but otherwise you'll have to.
- As I said before, you must be familiar with all the configurations from the earlier section of the course on Databricks cluster, so I won't repeat explaining all the configurations and just highlight the ones which are different when you're working with Unity Catalog.
- So before you start setting up your uh, unity catalog or your data governance layer or your storage layer for your project, you should already have at least one Databricks workspace, because half of the activity is done from the Databricks Workspace.
- For example, Azure external volumes represent existing data in the cloud storage that's managed outside of Databricks but registered in Databricks Unity Catalog so that Unity Catalog controls access to the storage via Databricks.
- But since you have already created a external location for this URL, external location name is this and there is a credential already mapped with this URL which allows, uh, Databricks Unity Catalog to access this, uh, location.
- The Architecture of Databricks Unity Catalog is getting closer to traditional database management systems where the users are expected to interact with the tables rather than the physical storage in the files themselves.
- In this lecture we will learn how to bring your corporate users or your domain users into Databricks Unity Catalog so that we can create user groups and manage privileges for those user groups or individual users.
- So you go over to the Databricks service and then under monitoring you will find Diagnostic settings and then you need to add the Diagnostic settings and you pick Databricks Unity Catalog from the category here.
- This Databricks workspace will now start using the Metastore from Unity Catalog and also you will get all the benefits that we talked about, such as Data Lineage, Audit Log, etc., that comes with Unity Catalog.
- You will be familiar with the Databricks cluster configurations from the earlier lessons, but there are some important things you need to bear in mind when you're creating clusters to work with Unity Catalog.
- Manage volumes are fully managed by Unity Catalog, which means that Unity Catalog manages access to the volume via Unity Catalog when you are in Databricks, as well as via the Cloud Provider account.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- So the other thing you can do to automate this entire process is that anytime a file is inserted into the S3 bucket, then you can send events notifications to a Lambda function, which will then trigger a Glue ETL job.
- So to understand that, let's narrow down a scenario and, uh, create a specific scenario which we can work out and along the way we will learn how to do it using declarative ETL tool DLT in Databricks.
- And then you have Glue Streaming ETL, and it's actually built on top of Apache Spark Structured Streaming, and instead of running ETL jobs, as you know, batch jobs you can run them as streaming jobs.
- And so therefore you can read data using Glue Streaming ETL from Kinesis Data Streams or Kafka or MSK as we'll see, which is managed Kafka on AWS.
- DLT simplifies infrastructure management by automating many of the operational complexities associated with running ETL pipelines.
- Then you would use the Glue ETL service to import the CSV and convert it into a Parquet format from within the Glue service.
- And we want to use the preview channel because unity catalog feature of, uh, this DLT pipeline is still in preview.
- You can use SQL or Python to instruct what needs to be done, and DLT takes care of how to perform the ETL.
- So in Databricks, DLT is a declarative ETL and it comes with an integrated orchestration capability.
- So Glue is a managed extract, transform and load service, also called commonly ETL service.
- So in DLT, in the declarative ETL, data quality checks are performed through expectations.
- Uh, but Databricks offers us a declarative ETL tool known as DLT or Delta live tables.

#### Module: Operational and Architecture Patterns
- Patterns matter when scale, governance, or reliability turns basic service use into system design.
- Prefer design implications and trade-offs over memorizing isolated features.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- The Architecture of Databricks Unity Catalog is getting closer to traditional database management systems where the users are expected to interact with the tables rather than the physical storage in the files themselves.
- Databricks considers the previous solution as legacy, and recommends using Unity Catalog for all new projects as it provides better data security and data governance.
- Uh, but Databricks offers us a declarative ETL tool known as DLT or Delta live tables.
- Even though hive Metastore was widely adopted and had been used by customers for over seven years, it has some limitations such as lack of fine grained access control like row and column level access, lack of audit logging, lineage support for multi-cloud environments, etc. so Databricks introduced Unity catalog around late 2022 to address some of the limitations of this solution.
- As I explained just now, the only difference is that the objects created under the hive Metastore catalog don't get the benefits offered by Unity Catalog, such as better governance, data lineage, data security, etc. so please refrain from using the hive Metastore for any new projects.
- As I said before, you must be familiar with all the configurations from the earlier section of the course on Databricks cluster, so I won't repeat explaining all the configurations and just highlight the ones which are different when you're working with Unity Catalog.
- So before you start setting up your uh, unity catalog or your data governance layer or your storage layer for your project, you should already have at least one Databricks workspace, because half of the activity is done from the Databricks Workspace.
- Now that we understand the project requirements at a high level and the Unity Catalog object model, let's take a look at the data architecture for our project so that we can create the necessary objects as we're using the medallion architecture.
- For example, Azure external volumes represent existing data in the cloud storage that's managed outside of Databricks but registered in Databricks Unity Catalog so that Unity Catalog controls access to the storage via Databricks.
- But since you have already created a external location for this URL, external location name is this and there is a credential already mapped with this URL which allows, uh, Databricks Unity Catalog to access this, uh, location.
- In this lecture we will learn how to bring your corporate users or your domain users into Databricks Unity Catalog so that we can create user groups and manage privileges for those user groups or individual users.
- So you go over to the Databricks service and then under monitoring you will find Diagnostic settings and then you need to add the Diagnostic settings and you pick Databricks Unity Catalog from the category here.

### Phase 2 - Core Services
- AWS Glue should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon RDS should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon Redshift should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon S3 should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon Athena should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.

### Phase 3 - Advanced Patterns
- Pattern emphasis: Analytical Storage and Query becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Consumption and BI becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Data Engineering Operational Context becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Distributed Processing becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Lakehouse Governance becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.

### Phase 4 - System Design (Production)
#### Reference Architecture
- Design lakehouse etl governance with explicit service boundaries, operational controls, and recovery expectations.
#### Scaling Considerations
- Scaling risk appears when lakehouse etl governance decisions are made service-by-service without a whole-system operating model.
#### Cost Considerations
- The main cost driver is usually misalignment between service choice, operations, and workload behavior.

## Hands-On Practice (Kid-Friendly Step-by-Step)

### Safety First
- Use a sandbox account only.
- Set a budget alert before creating resources.
- Delete resources after practice to avoid surprise cost.

### Service-Specific Lab Paths
### Activity 1: Amazon S3 Lab for Databricks, Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, Glue, and ETL governance patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> S3 -> Buckets
- Azure equivalent: Azure Blob Storage
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Storage
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> S3 -> Buckets.
3. Build: Create a bucket and upload one sample file.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Open object metadata and test download.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete objects and delete bucket.

### Activity 2: Amazon CloudWatch Lab for Databricks, Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, Glue, and ETL governance patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> CloudWatch
- Azure equivalent: Azure Monitor
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Monitoring
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> CloudWatch.
3. Build: Create one dashboard and one alarm.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Trigger alarm condition and inspect alarm history.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete dashboard and alarm.

### Activity 3: Amazon DynamoDB Lab for Databricks, Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, Glue, and ETL governance patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> DynamoDB -> Tables
- Azure equivalent: Azure Cosmos DB
- GCP equivalent: Firestore
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> DynamoDB -> Tables.
3. Build: Create a table with partition key and add one item.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Query the item using the partition key.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete test table.

### Activity 4: Amazon EC2 Lab for Databricks, Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, Glue, and ETL governance patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Instances
- Azure equivalent: Azure Virtual Machines
- GCP equivalent: Compute Engine
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Instances.
3. Build: Launch one small instance with default VPC.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Check instance running state and system status checks.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Terminate instance and remove extra volumes.

### Quick Troubleshooting
- Access denied: verify role permissions or ask admin for least-privilege lab policy.
- Resource limit reached: pick another region or request quota increase.
- Unexpected charges: stop/delete resources and review cost explorer immediately.

## Cross-Cloud Thinking Mode

Use this lens to study Databricks, Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, Glue, and ETL governance patterns in a cloud-agnostic way: focus on capability first, provider name second.

### Capability Mapping (AWS / Azure / GCP)
| Capability | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identity | IAM / IAM Identity Center | Microsoft Entra ID + RBAC | Cloud IAM |
| Object Storage | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| Virtual Machines | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| Serverless Functions | AWS Lambda | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions / Cloud Run |
| Containers | Amazon EKS / ECS | AKS | GKE / Cloud Run |
| Databases | RDS / DynamoDB / ElastiCache | Azure SQL / Cosmos DB / Azure Cache | Cloud SQL / Firestore / Memorystore |
| Networking | VPC / Route 53 / ELB | VNet / DNS / Load Balancer | VPC / Cloud DNS / Cloud Load Balancing |
| Observability | CloudWatch / X-Ray / CloudTrail | Azure Monitor / App Insights / Activity Log | Cloud Monitoring / Cloud Logging / Cloud Trace |

### Same Lab Across 3 Clouds
| Lab Theme | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amazon S3 | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| Amazon CloudWatch | Amazon CloudWatch | Azure Monitor | Cloud Monitoring |
| Amazon DynamoDB | Amazon DynamoDB | Azure Cosmos DB | Firestore |
| Amazon EC2 | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |

### How To Think Cross-Cloud
1. Start with workload requirement: latency, throughput, security, and compliance.
2. Map requirement to capability family (identity, network, compute, data, observability).
3. Compare service behavior, not branding: limits, scaling model, and failure modes.
4. Keep a portable architecture note: what is generic vs provider-specific.
5. Test same mini-lab in at least 2 clouds and write down differences.

### Child-Friendly Practice Challenge
- Pick one simple task (for example: upload one file, run one function, read one metric).
- Do the same task in AWS, Azure, and GCP.
- Fill this mini template:
  - What was the same?
  - What had a different name?
  - What felt easier/harder and why?

<!-- END PRACTICE_ENHANCEMENTS -->

## Cross-Domain Referrals

- No secondary referral sources were required for this topic.

- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query

Line 17:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query

- Key Insights: The data received then goes through the ETL or the Extract Transform Load process and then load it into a Data Warehouse or Data Marts.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

- Key Insights: Also, they are generally highly aggregated to provide meaningful business value such as Key Processing Indicators or KPIs.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI

Line 22:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They were very valuable for making business decisions and most large companies had at least one Data Warehouse by the early 2000s.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: At the same time, they had some significant issues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In early 2000s, due to the popularity of the Internet, the volume of data started to increase significantly and also the variety of their data had started to change as well.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We started to see unstructured data such as videos, images, text files, etc. and there were very valuable for making decisions.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But the Data Warehouses lacked support for the unstructured data we were seeing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In a Data Warehouse, the data was only loaded after the quality of the data has been checked and also once it has been transformed.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Dataflow and pipeline signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This meant it took longer to develop a solution to get new data into the Data Warehouse.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Data Warehouses were built on traditional relational databases or MPP solutions, which meant they used proprietary sort of file formats and resulted in vendor lock ins.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, the traditional on prem data warehouses were very difficult to scale or at times impossible.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This meant large data migration projects were required in order to scale up those databases.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Storage was expensive with large vendors and also it wasn't possible to scale up storage without compute, etc..
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Finally, Data Warehouses didn't provide sufficient support for Data Science or ML and AI workloads.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 36:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Data Lakes can not only handle structured and semi structured data, but they can also handle unstructured data, which is roughly about 90% of the data we are currently seeing.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This resulted in quicker timescales to develop solutions as well as fast ingestion times.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 41:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The Data Lakes were built on open source file formats such as Parquet, ORC or AVRO, which meant that we could use a wide variety of software and libraries to process and analyze the data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 42:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Data science and the Machine Learning workloads could use the raw data as well as the transformed data in the Data Lake.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 43:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But there was one major problem.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 44:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Data Lakes were too slow to service interactive BI reports, and there was lack of governance for the data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 45:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query, Consumption and BI, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So the industry moved towards copying the subset of the data from the Data Lake to the Data Warehouses, again to support these BI reports.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 46:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This resulted in a clunky architecture with too many moving parts.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 47:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now that how Data Lake Architecture looks, let's summarize the pitfalls of a Data Lake.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 48:
- Concepts: Distributed Processing, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Data Lakes offered no support for ACID transactions that cost a number of problems, failed jobs left partially loaded files in the Data Lakes, and they had to be cleaned up with separate processes during each rerun.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 49:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: There was no guarantee of consistent reads.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 50:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The users could be reading partially written data resulting in unreliable Data Lake.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 51:
- Concepts: Distributed Processing, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Handling a correction to the data being sent was a nightmare because Data Lakes offered no support for updates, so developers had to partition the data and rewrite the entire partition, in these circumstances, which resulted in increased development time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 52:
- Concepts: Distributed Processing, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: There was no support by the Data Lake to roll back any data being written, we either had to rewrite a partition or rewrite an entire table.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 53:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As part of the GDPR or the General Data Protection Regulation from European Union that users had the right to be foregone.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 54:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In order to implement this, companies had to delete that individual's data from the Data Lake.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 55:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Because the Data Lake offered no support for deletions, at times, entire files had to be rewritten after filtering the individual's data out.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 56:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Date Lakes offer no history or versioning of the data, making it difficult to do rollbacks as well as the governance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 57:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Combination of all these factors resulted in unreliable data swamps.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 58:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query, Consumption and BI, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: On top of this Data Lakes offered poor performance for interactive query and also poor BI support in terms of performance as well as security, governance, etc.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 59:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon Lex
- Key Insights: As we've seen so far, they were complex to setup.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 60:

- Services: AWS Batch, AWS Lambda, Amazon Lex

Line 61:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: A point to note here.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 62:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design, Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 63:

- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In summary, Data Warehouses were very good at dealing with BI workloads, but they lacked support for streaming, Data Science and Machine Learning workloads.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 64:

- Key Insights: Data Lakes on the other hand, were mainly focused on Data Science and Machine Learning workloads, but they fell short of satisfying the traditional BI workloads and they supported streaming workloads, but it was difficult to combine streaming and batch workloads.

### File: outputs/Data Engineering/azure-databricks-spark-core-for-data-engineers/25_Unity Catalog - Introduction/173_Unity Catalog Object Model Overview.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Welcome back.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Distributed Processing, Lakehouse Governance

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In this lesson, we are going to look at Unity Catalog Object Model and how to reference objects such as Tables and Views in your notebooks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We are now familiar with the Unity Catalog Metastore and created one of these in the previous lesson.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is the top level container for all our metadata in the Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It lives at the Databricks account level and you can create one per region and one or more Databricks workspaces can be attached to the metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's also paired with a container in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 first default storage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: All other objects hangs off this metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Catalog is a new concept within Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In the previous sections of the course, you wouldn't have seen catalogs as we were using legacy high Metastore rather than the Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Catalog is just a logical container within the metastore to organize your data assets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, you may decide to have one catalog per business unit or one per development environment.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Each Catalog may contain one or more schemas or databases.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They're just the next level containers within Catalogs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Schemas and Databases are synonymous within Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In the past, most of the documentation referred to this as database, but now the recommendation is to use the term schema.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is just to avoid any confusion with database systems and platforms.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I can see changes to this effect in the documentation too, so please start using the term Schema rather than Database.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you use database in SQL statements, for example, create database instead of create schema, it will still work, but I would encourage you to use schema.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Each Schema may contain one or more Tables, Views or Functions.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: There are no changes between Legacy Hive Metastore and Unity Catalog in this respect.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As before, there are managed as well as external tables in Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I assume you are familiar with managed and external tables by now, but a quick recap is that managed tables are basically owned by Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What this means is that Databricks manages both metadata and the data for the table, whereas only metadata for external tables are managed by Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The underlying data is managed by the Cloud Object Store.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when you drop an external table, only the table definitions are dropped.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The underlying data is left alone.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So they're great for when another solution, such as Azure Data Factory, is producing the data and Databricks is just consuming the data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In this example, you just create an external table on the data produced by ADF and consume within Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Having said that, there are some important changes to the way managed tables work in Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Firstly, all managed tables in Unity Catalog are Delta tables.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You cannot create a table with format Parquet, JSON, CSV, etc., as Managed Tables.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For that you will have to use External Tables.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS Config
- Key Insights: Second, by default, the data for Managed Tables will be written to the default storage used to configure the metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you remember, it used to be the DBFS root in Hive Metastore, but now it's the storage account we attached to the metastore while creation.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can change this to a different location by specifying a managed location in your create schema or create catalog statements.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In Hive Metastore when you drop a managed table, the underlying data is deleted immediately.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But in Unity Catalog enabled workspace, the data will be retained for 30 days.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 20:

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 22:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, each storage credential can be assigned access control policies to ensure that the right users and groups have got access.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 24:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: External location is an object that combines the storage credential with the Cloud Object Store, which is an Azure Data lake storage Gen2 in the case of Azure.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 26:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 27:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This way Unity Catalog provides more fine grained access control.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The answer is that if we have the manage location for the schema that takes precedence and that will be used otherwise, Unity Catalog will look for the manage location in the catalog and if there is one assigned that will be used.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

Line 22:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I personally prefer keeping data for each of the schemas in separate containers or folders in the Data Lake.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This helps me with architectural decisions such as capacity planning, for example, it's a lot easier to identify the size of a processing layer such as bronze, silver or gold when you keep the data separate.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, any kind of migration also becomes a lot easier.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: There might be use cases for which you may want to keep the data together in one container at the catalog level, but I haven't come across them, so I always tend to use the manage location for the schema and that's what we'll be doing for this project.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In our project we have three layers bronze, silver and gold.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance

- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, we want to know how it's changed or transformed within its journey.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And finally, we would want to know the destination of the data so that we can be confident about its usage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Data Lineage offers all three of these.

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In this lesson, we are going to look at Data Access Control capabilities offered by Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Unity catalog Security Model allows us to secure every single object that exists in the Metastore hierarchy.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Let's take a look at who we are securing the Metastore objects against.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: There are three types of databricks identities you can secure the object against.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: First one is Users.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: User is someone like you and me who logs into the user interface using the email address to interact with databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next one is the Service Principle.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Service Principle in Azure or application objects created in the Active Directory, they can be considered a system users created to help with automation of tasks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Managing access to every user in a team separately can be challenging and error prone.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So Databricks recommends using groups.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Groups can include one or more users or Service Principals.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It is recommended to use groups where possible.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: By default, Databricks employs an access control list or ACL Based Access Control model to secure the objects in Unity catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But there are some exceptions to this for the users based on their roles.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's take a look at that first and then we'll talk about ACL Based Security Model.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: A User in Unity catalog can take one of these four roles.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Account Admin is the highest level user in Unity catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They have full access to every metastore associated to the Databricks account.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They're the only ones who can create a Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: When a Metastore is created, they become the Metastore admin by default of the newly created Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But they can then assign another user or a group as the Metastore admin for the metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Metastore admins will have similar capabilities to account administrator, but they will only have access to the metastore, they are the administrator for, whereas account admin will have full access to every metastore in the Databricks account.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Metastore admins will also be able to transfer ownership of objects in the metastore as well as able to delete the metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Each object within the Metastore has a Owner.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: By default, the Owner is the User or the Service Principal who created the particular object.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But the ownership can be transferred to another user by the current owner, or either the Metastore administrator or the account administrator.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Owners will have full access to the object without any explicit permission being granted.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:

- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 62:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

Line 63:

Line 64:

Line 65:

Line 66:

Line 67:

Line 68:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

Line 69:

Line 70:

Line 71:

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 43:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Even though hive Metastore was widely adopted and had been used by customers for over seven years, it has some limitations such as lack of fine grained access control like row and column level access, lack of audit logging, lineage support for multi-cloud environments, etc. so Databricks introduced Unity catalog around late 2022 to address some of the limitations of this solution.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 44:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 45:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We can also create functions to abstract transformation logic as required.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Dataflow and pipeline signal.

- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 47:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's the recommended approach for reading and writing files in Lakehouse, especially files containing unstructured data.

- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance, Pipeline and Ingestion Design

Line 24:

Line 25:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Manage volumes are fully managed by Unity Catalog, which means that Unity Catalog manages access to the volume via Unity Catalog when you are in Databricks, as well as via the Cloud Provider account.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, Azure external volumes represent existing data in the cloud storage that's managed outside of Databricks but registered in Databricks Unity Catalog so that Unity Catalog controls access to the storage via Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is typically used for containers in which we receive data from external applications other than Databricks, but we want to use that data in Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Tables, as you know, are collections of data organized by rows and columns.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Again, tables can be both external and managed.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Managed tables are basically owned by Databricks Unity catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What this means is that Unity Catalog manages both the metadata and the data for the table, whereas only metadata for external tables are managed by Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The underlying data itself is managed by the cloud provider.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when you drop an external table, only the table definitions are dropped because that is what is managed by Databricks Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The underlying data itself is left alone, so they are great for when another solution, such as Azure Data Factory or Fivetran, is producing the data and Databricks is just consuming the data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In this example, you just need to create an external table on the data produced by ADF or patron and consume that data within Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, all managed tables in unity catalog are delta tables.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can't create a table with a format parquet, CSV or JSON, etc. as managed tables.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For that you will have to use external tables.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Please don't worry if you still haven't understood the difference.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I'll demonstrate this with an example in later lessons.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 41:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The objects called storage, credential, and external location allow us to access a cloud storage or a data lake other than the default storage attached to the Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 42:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Service credentials are used to hold the credentials of external cloud services altogether, for example an AWS service.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 43:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Similarly, connections represent credentials that give read only access to an external database in a database system such as MySQL or PostgreSQL.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 44:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can then access the MySQL database using the connection that you've stored within Unity Catalog via Lakehouse Federation.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 45:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: There are three other further objects called share recipient and provider, introduced by Unity Catalog to handle Delta sharing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 46:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And finally, using Unity Catalog for your Databricks workspace doesn't mean you can't access Legacy Hive Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 47:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Databricks offers 100% backward compatibility with hive Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 48:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is achieved via the pseudo catalog called hive Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 49:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This pseudo catalog is automatically created by Databricks in every Databricks workspace, and you can simply create your databases or schemas, as well as tables, views, and functions in hive Metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 50:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Similar to creating them via Unity Catalog, it also offers two types of tables external and managed.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 51:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As we discussed with Unity Catalog.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 52:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: With this hierarchical object structure, you will have to use the three level namespace to access a table or view, or a function.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 53:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, to access a table you will refer to catalog dot schema, dot table name.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 54:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you would access legacy tables in hive metastore as hive Metastore dot schema dot table.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 55:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If a Databricks workspace is not enabled with hive Metastore, you could use the two level namespace because the hive Metastore catalog itself is your default catalog, so you would just use the schema name dot table name.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 56:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But when you enable a Databricks workspace with unity catalog, you will have to use the three level namespace.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 57:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As I explained just now, the only difference is that the objects created under the hive Metastore catalog don't get the benefits offered by Unity Catalog, such as better governance, data lineage, data security, etc. so please refrain from using the hive Metastore for any new projects.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Governance and lineage signal.

Line 58:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: All of this may feel a bit overwhelming at first if you are new to Databricks, but please don't worry, I'll take you through each one of these objects you need to know for the certification exam in detail in the forthcoming lessons.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 59:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And that's the end of this lesson.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 60:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I'll see you in the next one.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

### File: outputs/Data Engineering/databricks-certified-data-engineer-associate-ultimate-prep/05_Introduction to Unity Catalog/029_Configure Access to Cloud Storage - Lecture.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Welcome back.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now that we have created the Unity Catalog, Metastore, let's take a look at how to read and write data from the cloud storage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As we discussed, we do not want to assign a default storage for the entire Metastore, because that may result in all the data for the Metastore being written to a single container, which makes it harder to maintain.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Instead, we want to assign specific storage containers for each of the catalogs or even schemas within the catalogs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In order to do that, we need to create the Unity Catalog objects.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Storage credential and external location.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Storage credential is simply an authentication authorization mechanism for accessing the data stored in the Azure storage on behalf of the users.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It can be created with the managed identity or a service principal.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you are new to Azure Managed Identity and Service Principal or just mechanisms for authenticating and authorizing Azure resources securely without needing to manage the credentials manually.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: External location simply combines the storage credential with the cloud storage container to gain access to the specific container in the cloud storage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We can create as many external locations as we need for the catalogs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, we can create subfolders within the container.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The external location refers to and assign them for each of the catalogs or schemas.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This way, the data for each of the catalogs or schemas can be kept separate and organized.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Let's now take a look at how to implement access to a cloud storage using the Unity Catalog objects, storage, credential and external location.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As we said, we can create a storage credential using a managed identity or a service principal to make it even easier.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Azure offers a first party service called Access Connector for Databricks, which lets us connect a managed identity to a Databricks account.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's very simple to implement, so we'll use that.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Once we create the access connector we need to assign the role storage blob data contributor to the access connector for the data lake so that the access connector can access the data lake.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We'll then create the storage credential using the access connector information.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: With that, the storage credential will also be able to access the data lake via the access connector.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We then need to create the external location.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As we said, external location is an object that combines storage credential and the ADLs storage container.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when an user references the external location, Unity catalog knows which storage credential to use for access, and if the storage credential has access to the storage account, the authentication succeeds.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can apply access control to both storage credential and the external location.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This lets the administrators manage access to the storage at a more granular level.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If an user doesn't have access to the storage credential or the external location, the request fails and Unity Catalog doesn't attempt to authenticate.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Governance and lineage signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That's everything you need to know.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But please note that unfortunately, the keyword external has slightly different meaning in different places in Databricks.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In the case of external location, external simply refers to a storage other than the default storage attached to the metastore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I appreciate that was a lot of information to take in by implementing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is quite simple.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's summarize the steps we need to carry out to implement this.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: First we need to create the access connector.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You may already have one in your subscription created by Databricks by default.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can either use that or create a new one.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I'm going to create a new one just for this part of the course to keep it separate.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's best to keep it separate so that you can manage access separately if required.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next, we need to create an Azure Data Lake storage account.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Again, you may already have a data lake in your subscription already created by Databricks, but Databricks manages access to that storage account, so we won't use that.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 41:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Instead, we will create a new one for which we'll have full access.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 42:
- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We then need to assign the storage blob data contributor role on the data lake to the access connector.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 43:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next we need to create the storage credential using the access connector information.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 44:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: With that we would have completed everything to do with the storage credential.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 45:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next we need to create the external location.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 46:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We'll then be able to access the data in the storage account via the external location and the storage credential.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As we are creating these resources ourselves via the owners, and we will have full access to the storage, credential, external location, etc. but if you want to grant access to the rest of the team, you can do that as well.

- Concepts: Lakehouse Governance

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Firstly, materialized views used to be called live tables, and the syntax for creating a materialized view was a create or refresh live table instead of create or refresh materialized view.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, if you run either of those statements, that will create a materialized view.

Line 9:

Line 10:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design, Streaming State and Time

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design, Streaming State and Time
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Again, both statements today means the same thing and they simply create a streaming table.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI

Line 13:

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Please be aware of this.

Line 15:

Line 16:
